channel.title: Posts on codedinsugar.com
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on codedinsugar.com
channel.copyright: This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License.

item.title: Retrieve hyperlinks via cli with shuttle
item.description: To infinity and beyond. -Buzz Lightyear I spend a lot of time at the terminal, either in vscode or a zsh shell. Two apps I use often are tldr and cht.sh because: I don’t remember the bookmarks that I have. I have too many bookmarks. While tldr and cht.sh are handy for retrieving public info, I needed a similar utility for work. Our tech stack has a lot of astronomy jargon e.
item.content:
To infinity and beyond.

-Buzz Lightyear

I spend a lot of time at the terminal, either in vscode or a zsh shell.

Two apps I use often are tldr and cht.sh because:

  1. I don’t remember the bookmarks that I have.
  2. I have too many bookmarks.

While tldr and cht.sh are handy for retrieving public info, I needed a similar utility for work. Our tech stack has a lot of astronomy jargon e.g. galaxy, quadrant, planet, etc. and since we embrace a DevOps culture I wanted to build something that blends with our spatial theme and also fits nicely with multiple teams.

Introducting shuttle - a simple cli tool to retrieve hyperlinks.

Usage is fairly simple, just run ./shuttle $arg and it’ll retrieve the contents of a respectively named json file.

Example:

#microservice1.json is in the shuttle-files path

./shuttle microservice1
{
  "name": "microservice1",
  "wiki": "https://microservice1/wiki",
  "playbook": "https://microservice1/playbook",
  "diagram": "https://microservice1/diagram",
  "communication": "https://microservice1/comms"
}

Check it out here!

item.title: How'd I Get Here? GitHub + Hugo + Namecheap
item.description: A man has no name. -Jaqen H’ghar I consider myself a private person, especially my digital persona. I don’t do social media. The last public internet presence I updated was probably my LinkedIn account some 10 years ago and a short-lived MySpace account almost 20 years ago. Surprisingly my Flickr account is still active and I’ll ask an occassional question on Reddit but that’s about it. Call me old-skool but I prefer to keep my interactions in-person.
item.content:
A man has no name.

-Jaqen H’ghar

I consider myself a private person, especially my digital persona. I don’t do social media. The last public internet presence I updated was probably my LinkedIn account some 10 years ago and a short-lived MySpace account almost 20 years ago. Surprisingly my Flickr account is still active and I’ll ask an occassional question on Reddit but that’s about it. Call me old-skool but I prefer to keep my interactions in-person.

However, I do believe in good documentation. Even if you’re the only person who will ever look at your docs, write it down!

Whomever should stumble upon this post, this is how I provisioned this blog.

GitHub Pages is your friend

The Hugo team has a good doc on hosting a blog on GitHub Pages here, but this is what I did:

  1. From github.com create two public repositories: blog and codedinsugar.github.io. The first repo name is arbitrary of course, but the second repo name has to be in the syntax of <github username>.github.io. Obviously for the sake of this tutorial, just substitute codedinsugar for whatever your github username is.
  2. From wherever your typewriter sits, clone both repos above to their own paths:
cd <arbitrary-path>
git clone https://github.com/codedinsugar/blog.git
git clone https://github.com/codedinsugar/codedinsugar.github.io.git
  1. Browse to the local blog repo and create a new hugo site:
cd blog
hugo new site blog
  1. Browse to the local codedinsugar.github.io repo and create a main branch, add a README.md file, commit and push. This is where the static content will live.
cd codedinsugar.github.io
git checkout -b main
touch README.md
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit for static content"
git push origin main
  1. Browse back to the local blog repo. Delete the blog/public folder if it already exists (should be empty). Add the local codedinsugar.github.io repo as a submodule to a new blog/public folder:
cd blog
rm -rf blog/public
git submodule add -b main https://github.com/codedinsugar/codedinsugar.github.io.git public

  1. Pick and use a Hugo theme. I went with Hermit but I’m not pointing to it directly at the blog/themes path. Instead I just cloned the theme outside of the local blog path, looked at the tree structure and copied the relevant files to the local blog repo. I don’t have a theme="Hermit" variable in my config.toml file either. This method is cleaner for me because if I’m going to make changes then I don’t want to have to depend on an external theme repo. If you choose to use a theme directly, read about it here

  2. Start creating that sweet content in the local blog repo:

cd blog
hugo new posts/foo.md

#file is created at blog/content/posts/foo.md
  1. Run the local hugo server to make sure everything builds, renders successfully, and most it passes an initial satisfaction test:
hugo server -D

#browse to http://localhost:1313
  1. Build all binaries which get published to blog/public:
hugo
  1. Add, commit, and push the static content to GitHub:
cd public #remember this is a submodule to the static content repo
git add .
git commit -m "Adding first post"
git push origin main
  1. Browse to https://github.com/codedinsugar/codedinsugar.github.io and verify your commits.
  2. Verify HTTPS is enabled for public codedinsugar repo > Settings > Pages > Enforce HTTPS.
  3. Browse to https://codedinsugar.github.io and enjoy your new site.
  4. If you don’t intend to use custom domain redirection to point to your site then you’re done here, otherwise see next section for Namecheap setup.

Namecheap is also your friend

Namecheap has always been my go to domain registrar for several years. They’re an ICANN accredited registrar and I’ve never had an issue with their services. Getting started is pretty simple, create an account and buy a domain name. Don’t worry about buying an SSL certificate from Namecheap since GitHub Pages will be handling HTTPS.

Namecheap has a knowledgebase article on how to link your custom domain with GitHub Pages which is exactly what I followed verbatim.

After creating all of the necessary DNS records, head back to your public GitHub Pages repo > Settings > Pages > Custom domain and add your apex domain here e.g. codedinsugar.com. If the DNS check fails, then you’re likely missing a CNAME (case sensitive) file in your public repo. The value in the CNAME record should be just your apex domain without any http(s):// prefix.

There really isn’t much to pointing a custom domain to GitHub Pages, they even have a troubleshooting guide here if you get stuck. Good luck!

item.title: Blog Roadmap
item.description: Before the score comes the goal July 2022 Intro posts August 2022 Configure a light|dark|colored mode September 2022 Add a custom logo and a main page image October 2022 Persist the navigation menu (current styling auto hides it) November 2022 Maintain a one post per week minimum standard December 2022 End of year wrap up
item.content:
Before the score comes the goal

July 2022

  • Intro posts

August 2022

  • Configure a light|dark|colored mode

September 2022

  • Add a custom logo and a main page image

October 2022

  • Persist the navigation menu (current styling auto hides it)

November 2022

  • Maintain a one post per week minimum standard

December 2022

  • End of year wrap up
